items.title: Classification of abdominal trauma
items.contentHtml:
Classification of abdominal trauma

  • Penetrating trauma: stab wounds (HAB) or firearm (HAF); the peritoneal cavity is violated if there is a break in the continuity of the parietal peritoneum.
  • Blunt trauma: high-energy mechanism (vehicular accident, fall from a height, assault); It produces injuries due to compression, deceleration or burst.
  • According to the AAST (American Association for the Surgery of Trauma), injuries to solid organs (I to V) and hollow viscus (I to V) are graded.
  • Anatomical areas: anterior abdomen (from xiphoid appendix to iliac crests and inguinal folds), flanks and back.
  • The triad of death (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy) conditions surgical management for damage control.

items.title: Penetrating vs blunt mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Mechanism of penetrating vs blunt injury

  • Penetrating: HAB produces direct laceration injury; HAF causes temporary and permanent cavitation due to kinetic energy (E = 1/2 mv²).
  • Blunt: sudden deceleration (e.g. seat belt) vascular pedicles (spleen, kidney); compression against the vertebral column (pancreas, duodenum).
  • In blunt trauma, 25% of patients with hollow viscus injury present late peritonitis (>6 hours).
  • Diaphragmatic injury is more common in penetrating (left thoracoabdominal) than in blunt trauma.
  • Stab wound rule: if the fascia is intact, there is no significant intra-abdominal injury in 98% of cases.

items.title: Indications for urgent laparotomy
items.contentHtml:
Indications for urgent laparotomy

  • Persistent hypotension (SBP < 90 mmHg) after initial resuscitation with 2 L of crystalloids.
  • Signs of peritoneal irritation (guarding, rigidity, pain on decompression).
  • Evisceration or penetrating wound with blood. active by nasogastric or rectal tube.
  • Pneumoperitoneum on plain x-ray or CT (Rigler's sign).
  • Positive diagnostic peritoneal lavage (DPL): erythrocyte count > 100,000/mm³ or leukocytes > 500/mm³.
  • Positive FAST with instability hemodynamics (free fluid in hepatorenal, splenorenal, pelvic or pericardial window).

items.title: Signs of peritoneal irritation
items.contentHtml:
Signs of peritoneal irritation

  • Pain on decompression (Blumberg): sign of parietal peritoneal irritation; sensitivity 80% for peritonitis.
  • Involuntary muscle defense: persistent abdominal contracture that does not subside with distraction.
  • Psoas sign: pain on passive hip extension (retroperitoneal irritation due to appendicitis or abscess).
  • Murphy sign: interruption of inspiration due to pain on right subcostal palpation. (acute cholecystitis).
  • In trauma, frank peritonitis indicates hollow viscus injury (perforation) or massive hemoperitoneum.
  • The absence of peritoneal signs does not rule out retroperitoneal injury (duodenum, ascending/descending colon).

items.title: Assessment with FAST in trauma
items.contentHtml:
Assessment with FAST in trauma

  • FAST (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma): ultrasound aimed at detecting intra-abdominal (hemoperitoneum) and pericardial free fluid.
  • Windows evaluated: hepatorrenal (Morrison), splenorenal, pelvis (Douglas) and pericardial (subxiphoid).
  • Sensitivity for hemoperitoneum in blunt trauma: 85-95%; specificity > 95%.
  • Does not replace CT in stable patients; in unstable, positive FAST is an indication for urgent laparotomy.
  • False negative: isolated retroperitoneal injury, hollow viscus without fluid, diaphragmatic injury without effusion.
  • False positive: pre-existing ascites, peritoneal dialysis fluid.

items.title: Triangle of death in trauma
items.contentHtml:
Triangle of death in trauma

  • Triangle of death: hypothermia (T < 35°C), acidosis (pH < 7.2) and coagulopathy (INR > 1.5 or PT < 50%).
  • Pathophysiology: hypoperfusion → lactic acidosis; heat loss → platelet and enzyme dysfunction; consumption of factors → dilutional and consumptive coagulopathy.
  • Indicates need for damage control surgery: abbreviated laparotomy, packing, temporary closure.
  • Management: resuscitation with 1:1:1 (concentrated red blood cells, fresh frozen plasma, platelets), active warming, tranexamic acid 1 g in bolus followed by 1 g in 8 hours.
  • Avoid excessive crystalloid fluids (> 2 L) to avoid aggravating coagulopathy and hypothermia.

items.title: Spleen injuries: conservative management
items.contentHtml:
Spleen injuries and conservative management

  • The spleen is the most frequently injured organ in blunt trauma (40-55%).
  • AAST grading: I (subcapsular hematoma <10% surface), II (hematoma 10-50%, laceration 1-3 cm), III (hematoma >50%, laceration >3 cm), IV (segmental or hilar laceration), V (fragmentation or total vascular injury).
  • Conservative management criteria (non-operative): hemodynamically stable, grade ≤ III, without associated hollow viscus injury, without active bleeding on CT.
  • Failure of conservative management: transfusion requirement > 2 U in 24 h or instability.
  • Post-splenectomy vaccination: Pneumococcus, Meningococcus, Haemophilus influenzae type b (ideally 2 weeks before).

items.title: Liver injuries: gradation
items.contentHtml:
Liver injuries and grading

  • The liver is the second most injured organ in blunt trauma (15-20%).
  • Hepatic AAST grading: I (subcapsular hematoma <10% surface, laceration <1 cm depth), II (hematoma 10-50%, laceration 1-3 cm), III (hematoma >50%, laceration >3 cm), IV (parenchymal laceration 25-75% of lobe), V (retrohepatic vascular injury or fragmentation), VI (hepatic avulsion).
  • Initial management: non-operative in stable patient, grades I-IV if there is no active bleeding (embolization if extravasation).
  • Grade V injuries require surgery (packing, repair vascular).
  • Complication: Budd-Chiari syndrome due to compression of suprahepatic veins.

items.title: Hollow viscus injuries and peritonitis
items.contentHtml:
Hollow viscus injuries and peritonitis

  • Hollow viscus most common in penetrating trauma: small intestine (50%); in blunt: duodenum and colon.
  • Chemical peritonitis due to gastric juice or biliary; bacterial peritonitis due to colonic content.
  • Diagnosis: CT with oral/intravenous contrast shows pneumoperitoneum, parietal thickening or contrast extravasation.
  • Rigler's sign: free gas on both sides of the intestinal wall visible on radiography.
  • LPD: leukocytes > 500/mm³ suggests perforation of hollow viscus.
  • Management: exploratory laparotomy, primary repair or resection with anastomosis (if minimal contamination and stable patient).

items.title: Diaphragmatic injury diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis of diaphragmatic injury

  • More common in left thoracoabdominal penetrating trauma (70-90% on the left side).
  • Key finding on chest x-ray: elevation of the hemidiaphragm, herniation of abdominal viscus (stomach, colon) or sign of hump.
  • Multidetector CT: collar sign (narrowing of the herniated viscus when passing through the defect) or dependent viscus sign (supported by the posterior chest wall).
  • If in doubt, diagnostic laparoscopy or thoracoscopy; sensitivity > 95%.
  • Right diaphragmatic injury more difficult to detect (liver protects); suggests associated hepatic subcapsular hematoma.
  • Surgical repair is always indicated (abdominal route preferred to explore other injuries).

items.title: Late complications: intra-abdominal abscess
items.contentHtml:
Late complications: intra-abdominal abscess

  • Occurs in 5-15% of patients with abdominal trauma, especially after colon or pancreas injury.
  • Pathophysiology: persistent bacterial contamination, infected hematoma or anastomotic leak.
  • Clinics: persistent fever (> 38.5°C), leukocytosis, ileus prolonged (> 5 days).
  • Diagnosis: CT with intravenous contrast showing fluid collection with wall enhancement (bubble sign if gas).
  • Management: CT-guided percutaneous drainage (success > 80%) plus antibiotics (piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g every 6 h or carbapenem).
  • If percutaneous drainage or multiloculated abscess fails → laparotomy and surgical drainage.

items.foco: Management of renal trauma and retroperitoneal hematoma
items.title: Management of renal trauma and retroperitoneal hematoma
items.contentHtml:
Management of renal trauma and retroperitoneal hematoma

  • Renal trauma: blunt in 90% of cases; grade I-II (AAST) are managed conservatively (rest, hydration, monitoring of vital signs).
  • Retroperitoneal hematoma: zone I (central) suggests injury to the aorta, vena cava, pancreas or duodenum → mandatory exploratory laparotomy; zone II (flanks) → explore whether expansion or rupture; zone III (pelvis) → conservative management if stable (pelvic fracture).
  • Indications for kidney surgery: hemodynamic instability, grade V laceration (pedicle avulsion), pulsatile hematoma in zone II.
  • Intraoperative intravenous urography: if kidney injury, 2 mL/kg of contrast; visualization of both kidneys.
  • Nephrectomy only if kidney is non-viable or uncontrollable bleeding.
